article.title: UC San Diego's CRISPR Gene Drive Spreads Through Bacteria to Dismantle Antibiotic Resistance
article.summary: A second-generation CRISPR system that propagates itself through bacterial populations via conjugal transfer has demonstrated the ability to actively reverse antibiotic resistance, rather than merely slow its spread.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Researchers at the University of California San Diego have developed a self-propagating CRISPR-based system that spreads through bacterial populations to disable antibiotic resistance genes. Published in *npj Antimicrobials and Resistance* on February 18, 2026, the technology — called pPro-MobV — marks a significant departure from existing approaches, which largely focus on slowing or managing antibiotic resistance rather than reversing it.

The work arrives at a critical moment. According to a [2024 analysis in The Lancet](https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(24)01867-1/fulltext), bacterial antimicrobial resistance is projected to be directly responsible for up to 39 million deaths between 2025 and 2050, with annual death tolls rising steeply as resistance genes proliferate through hospital, agricultural, and environmental settings.

## What We Know

The pPro-MobV system is described as a second-generation Pro-Active Genetics (Pro-AG) tool, adapting concepts from insect gene-drive research — where an engineered gene propagates itself through a population — to the bacterial context. As reported by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/02/260217005717.htm), the cassette uses conjugal transfer, a bacterial mating-like process, to move CRISPR components from one cell to another. Once inside a host cell, the cassette targets antibiotic resistance genes carried on plasmids — small circular DNA elements that replicate independently and are a primary vector for spreading resistance — and disrupts their function.

Professor Ethan Bier of UC San Diego's School of Biological Sciences, who led the research alongside Professor Justin Meyer and Professor Victor Nizet from the School of Medicine, explained the population-scale logic to [phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-02-crispr-tool-bacteria-disable-antibiotic.html): "With pPro-MobV we have brought gene-drive thinking from insects to bacteria as a population engineering tool. With this new CRISPR-based technology we can take a few cells and let them go to neutralize AR in a large target population."

The system demonstrated several properties that increase its potential practical utility:

- **Biofilm penetration**: pPro-MobV proved effective within bacterial biofilms — the protective, surface-adhering communities that shield bacteria from conventional antibiotic treatment and are implicated in the majority of serious, treatment-resistant infections.
- **Phage delivery compatibility**: The CRISPR cassette's components can be packaged into engineered bacteriophages, viruses that naturally target bacteria, enabling targeted delivery to specific bacterial populations.
- **Built-in reversibility**: The team incorporated a safety mechanism based on homology-based deletion, which allows the gene cassette to be removed from the population if the intervention is deemed problematic — functioning as a controllable kill switch for the spreading element.

Meyer underscored the distinction from existing tools in comments reported by [phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-02-crispr-tool-bacteria-disable-antibiotic.html): "This technology is one of the few ways that I'm aware of that can actively reverse the spread of antibiotic-resistant genes, rather than just slowing or coping with their spread."

## Context: Why This Research Matters

Antibiotic resistance operates on a fundamentally different timescale than most medical challenges. Resistance genes can spread across bacterial species, geographies, and environments within years, and the gene-containing plasmids that carry resistance traits move freely between unrelated bacterial strains through the same conjugal transfer mechanism that pPro-MobV now co-opts.

According to [The Lancet's systematic analysis](https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(24)01867-1/fulltext), more than one million people died annually attributable to bacterial antimicrobial resistance every year from 1990 through 2021, with bacterial AMR directly responsible for 1.27 million deaths in 2019 alone. Projections extending to 2050 suggest the direct death toll could climb to roughly 39 million over the 25-year period as resistance becomes more entrenched.

The pPro-MobV approach is notable precisely because it turns the resistance-spreading mechanism — plasmid-based conjugal transfer — against itself. Rather than attempting to outpace evolution with new antibiotics (a process that takes a decade and faces enormous regulatory and commercial barriers), the system attempts to alter the genetic composition of entire bacterial populations.

## Applications Under Investigation

As described by UC San Diego, potential deployment scenarios extend well beyond hospital infection control. The [UC San Diego news release](https://today.ucsd.edu/story/new-crispr-based-system-targets-amplified-antibiotic-resistant-genes) notes that researchers envision applications in environmental remediation — targeting resistance genes in sewage treatment systems, aquaculture facilities such as fish farms, and agricultural feedlots, where antibiotic use is heavy and resistance gene reservoirs are large.

Nizet, a Distinguished Professor at UC San Diego School of Medicine, noted that a phage-based delivery system could be "deployed to address" chronic infections such as those found in cystic fibrosis and tuberculosis patients, where bacterial persistence in biofilms is a defining clinical problem.

The foundation for this research extends back to 2019 work on the first-generation Pro-AG system, which demonstrated a 100-fold improvement over existing cut-and-destroy CRISPR methods and achieved approximately a 100,000-fold reduction in antibiotic-resistant bacteria in laboratory cultures of *E. coli* carrying ampicillin-resistance genes.

## What We Don't Know

The current pPro-MobV results are laboratory-based. Several significant unknowns remain before any real-world application:

- **Ecological specificity**: Conjugal transfer does not discriminate between targeted and non-targeted bacterial strains; unintended spread into commensal or beneficial bacterial populations would need to be characterized and controlled.
- **Resistance to the drive itself**: Bacteria may evolve resistance to pPro-MobV through mutations in the conjugal transfer machinery or the target plasmid sequences, potentially limiting the system's durability.
- **Regulatory pathway**: No regulatory framework currently exists for deploying self-propagating genetic elements in environmental or clinical settings. Gene drives in insects have faced extensive debate; a bacterial gene drive would face analogous — and potentially more complex — scrutiny.
- **Real-world bacterial community complexity**: Laboratory experiments use controlled populations. Hospital, agricultural, and environmental microbiomes contain thousands of interacting species, and the system's behavior in those ecosystems is unknown.

The research team has been explicit that significant additional work is required before clinical or environmental trials could begin, but characterizes pPro-MobV as establishing proof-of-concept for a fundamentally new class of antimicrobial intervention.
